full.answer:
The total net electrical output of a full 12-module NuScale plant is approximately 570 MWe.  
Source: nuscale_ch01_0006, nuscale_ch01_0007, nuscale_ch01_0008, nuscale_ch01_0005 (all p.9).

The NuScale Reactor Coolant System (RCS) has two reactor safety valves (RSVs).  
Source: Table 5.2-2 and Figure 5.1-2, NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report.

The NuScale RCS uses natural circulation, where heated coolant rises through the central riser above the core, creating a density difference that drives upward flow to the steam generators and downward flow through the annulus; the riser’s height maximizes this driving head, establishing and sustaining the natural circulation loop.  
(Source: nuscale_ch01_0018; nuscale_ch05_0003; Figures 5.1-1, 5.1-3, 1.2-7)

The NuScale seismic design in Ch.01 is described as meeting General Design Criterion 2 (GDC 2) and regulatory guidance for seismic and environmental qualification of safety-related structures, systems, and components, ensuring that RCS components are designed, fabricated, and tested to ASME BPVC Section III, 2013 Edition standards (Ch.05), thus maintaining the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary under seismic events (see nuscale_ch01_0312, nuscale_ch01_0150, nuscale_ch05_0008, nuscale_ch05_0007).

The NuScale natural circulation driving force is much lower than the forced circulation in a conventional PWR, as NuScale relies solely on passive density differences (no pumps), resulting in lower flow rates (NuScale: 587 kg/s at 100% power vs. typical PWR: several thousand kg/s), but the design compensates with a smaller core and lower power density; forced circulation in conventional PWRs uses powerful pumps to achieve much higher driving forces and flow rates.  
(Source: nuscale_ch01_0052, Table 1.3-1; nuscale_ch05_0003; nuscale_ch05_0006; Table 5.1-2)

The SG (Steam Generator) design parameters (Table 5.4-2) are: design pressure 2100 psia, design temperature 650°F, tube wall outer diameter 0.625 in, and total heat transfer area 17,928 ft²; the RPV (Reactor Pressure Vessel) design parameters (Table 5.3-1) are: design pressure 2100 psia, design temperature 650°F, overall height 778 in, and inside diameter (lower section) 96.5 in—both components share the same design pressure and temperature, but differ in geometry and function.  
(Source: Table 5.4-2 and Table 5.3-1)

The NuScale RPV has an inside diameter of 107.5 inches and an overall height of 778 inches, compared to a typical PWR RPV inside diameter of 173 inches, with NuScale’s design integrating the core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single, more compact vessel as shown in Figure 5.3-1 and detailed in Table 5.3-1 and Table 1.3-1.  
(Source: Table 1.3-1, Table 5.3-1, Figure 5.3-1)

Yes, the minimum design flow rate of 538.5 kg/s at 100% power is adequate for core cooling, as confirmed by the safety analysis and natural circulation discussion, which demonstrate compliance with thermal margin and critical heat flux criteria (Table 5.1-2; nuscale_ch05_0006; nuscale_ch01_0732; nuscale_ch01_0057).

Yes, the integral steam generator (SG) design described in both Ch.01 and Ch.05 is adequate for addressing steam generator tube rupture (SGTR) concerns, as it fully conforms to regulatory criteria for accident analysis, ensures robust pressure boundary integrity, and incorporates passive safety features and material specifications that meet or exceed NRC and ASME requirements (nuscale_ch01_0657, nuscale_ch01_0662, nuscale_ch05_0019, nuscale_ch05_0020, Table 5.4-2, Figures 5.4-1/2/3/6/7, Figure 1.2-7).

Yes, the interface between the primary and secondary systems is adequately designed, as the steam generators are fully integrated within the reactor pressure vessel with robust helical tube bundles, qualified materials, and isolation features, meeting regulatory and design requirements for integrity and safety (Figures 1.2-3, 5.4-1; nuscale_ch05_0020; Table 5.4-2; nuscale_ch05_0019).

no_vision.answer: The total net electrical output of a full 12-module NuScale plant is approximately 570 MWe after accounting for house loads (nuscale_ch01_0006; nuscale_ch01_0007; nuscale_ch01_0008; nuscale_ch01_0005).

The NuScale Reactor Coolant System (RCS) has two spring-loaded safety valves.  
Source: nuscale_ch01_0769 (NuScale FSAR, discussion of spring-loaded safety valves in place of PORVs).

The NuScale RCS uses natural circulation, where heated coolant rises through a central hot leg riser above the core, flows through the steam generators to transfer heat, and then descends as it cools, with the riser providing the necessary elevation and separation to establish and sustain this flow path. The riser’s role is to create a vertical height difference that drives the buoyancy-induced circulation essential for passive core cooling.  
Sources: nuscale_ch01_0018; nuscale_ch05_0003; nuscale_ch05_0019.

The NuScale seismic design in Ch.01 is described as fully conforming to GDC 2 and RG 1.100, requiring safety-related systems—including RCS components—to be seismically qualified to withstand design basis earthquakes, and this means all RCS pressure-retaining components in Ch.05 (such as the reactor vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping) are designed, fabricated, and tested to ASME BPVC Class 1 standards per 10 CFR 50.55a to ensure integrity under seismic events.  
Sources: nuscale_ch01_0307, nuscale_ch01_0150, nuscale_ch05_0008, nuscale_ch05_0007.

The NuScale reactor uses natural circulation, which provides a significantly lower driving force for coolant flow compared to the forced circulation produced by reactor coolant pumps in a conventional PWR; NuScale achieves core cooling without pumps, relying on system design to minimize pressure losses and maintain adequate flow, whereas conventional PWRs use high-capacity pumps to generate much greater flow rates and pressure heads.  
Source: [nuscale_ch05_0003], [nuscale_ch01_0052], [nuscale_ch01_0730], [nuscale_ch01_0742]

The SG (Steam Generator) design parameters from Table 5.4-1 show a lower design pressure (2,250 psia) and temperature (650°F) compared to the RPV (Reactor Pressure Vessel) design parameters from Table 5.3-1, which are 2,500 psia and 650°F, respectively; both components are integrated, but the RPV is designed for higher pressure.  
Source: nuscale_ch05_0019, nuscale_ch05_0020, nuscale_ch05_0014.

Table 1.3-1 shows the NuScale RPV has a height of 22.0 m and an inside diameter of 2.8 m, compared to a typical PWR RPV height of 13.7 m and inside diameter of 4.0 m; Table 5.3-1 and Figure 5.3-1 confirm the NuScale RPV dimensions as 22.0 m tall and 2.8 m in diameter.  
(Source: nuscale_ch01_0052, nuscale_ch05_0015, nuscale_ch05_0014)

Yes, the minimum design flow rate specified in Table 5.1-2 is adequate for core cooling, as confirmed by the text discussion and supporting critical heat flux testing, which demonstrate that natural circulation meets all regulatory thermal margin and fuel temperature limits for the NuScale design.  
(Source: nuscale_ch05_0006; nuscale_ch01_0732; nuscale_ch01_0057)

Yes, the integral steam generator (SG) design described in both Ch.01 and Ch.05 is adequate for addressing steam generator tube rupture (SGTR) concerns, as it meets regulatory requirements for radiological consequence analyses and incorporates design features and operational experience that support established tube plugging and degradation management criteria (nuscale_ch01_0662; nuscale_ch05_0020; nuscale_ch05_0021).

Yes, the interface between the primary and secondary systems is adequately designed, as Figures 1.2-3 and 5.4-1 show that the steam generators are integral to the reactor pressure vessel with the pressure boundary and leakage detection features meeting regulatory requirements for integrity and radiological safety (nuscale_ch05_0020; nuscale_ch05_0007; nuscale_ch01_0510).

no_edges.answer:
The total net electrical output of a full 12-module NuScale plant is approximately 570 MWe.  
Source: nuscale_ch01_0006, nuscale_ch01_0007, nuscale_ch01_0008, nuscale_ch01_0005 (all p.9).

The NuScale Reactor Coolant System (RCS) has 2 reactor safety valves.  
Source: Table 5.2-2 (lists "First valve" and "Second valve" with setpoints), Figure 5.1-2 (shows two RSVs in the RCS diagram).

The NuScale RCS uses natural circulation, where heated coolant rises through the core and riser due to density differences, then cools in the steam generator and descends in the downcomer; the riser provides the vertical height needed to establish the driving force for this circulation by maximizing the density difference between the hot and cold legs (see Figure 1.2-6 and nuscale_ch05_0003, nuscale_ch05_0004).

The NuScale seismic design in Ch.01 requires partial conformance to RG 1.100 for seismic and functional qualification of electrical and active mechanical equipment, and all RCS components are designed, fabricated, and tested to ASME BPVC Class 1 standards to meet seismic and safety requirements (nuscale_ch01_0150; nuscale_ch05_0008).

The NuScale reactor relies solely on natural circulation, which provides a much lower driving force for coolant flow compared to the forced circulation created by reactor coolant pumps in a conventional PWR; this is enabled by NuScale’s integrated design with low pressure losses and no external loops or pumps, as shown in Table 1.3-1 and described in [nuscale_ch05_0003] and [nuscale_ch01_0730].

The SG (Steam Generator) and RPV (Reactor Pressure Vessel) share the same design pressure (2100 psia) and temperature (650°F), but the RPV is much larger (overall height 778 in, inside diameter up to 106.5 in) compared to the SG tube bundle (tube OD 0.625 in, wall thickness 0.050 in, total heat transfer area 17,928 ft²); the SG is a helical, once-through type with 1380 tubes per module.  
Sources: Table 5.4-2, Table 5.3-1.

The NuScale RPV has an inside diameter of 107.5 inches and an overall height of 778 inches, compared to a typical PWR RPV with an inside diameter of 173 inches; NuScale’s RPV is significantly smaller and integrates the core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel as shown in Figure 5.3-1 and detailed in Table 5.3-1 and Table 1.3-1 (NuScale vs PWR parameters).  
Sources: Table 1.3-1, Table 5.3-1, Figure 5.3-1.

Yes, the minimum design flow rate of 538.5 kg/s at full power is adequate for core cooling, as confirmed by Table 5.1-2 and the text, which state that the natural circulation design maintains sufficient coolant flow to protect fuel integrity and meet safety margins under all operating conditions (Table 5.1-2; nuscale_ch05_0003; nuscale_ch05_0006).

Yes, the integral steam generator (SG) design described in both Ch.01 and Ch.05 is adequate for addressing steam generator tube rupture (SGTR) concerns, as it incorporates robust helical tube construction, novel support structures, and conforms to regulatory requirements for accident analysis and radiological consequences, with prototypic testing and standard SG program guidelines deemed sufficient for tube integrity and degradation management (nuscale_ch05_0021; nuscale_ch01_0657; nuscale_ch01_0662; Figures 5.4-1, 5.4-2, 5.4-6, 5.4-7).

Yes, the interface between the primary and secondary systems is adequately designed, as the steam generators are fully integrated within the reactor pressure vessel, forming robust pressure boundaries and utilizing materials and configurations (e.g., helical tubes, specified wall thicknesses, and clad layers) that meet ASME code requirements and ensure safe separation and heat transfer between systems (see Figure 1.2-3, Figure 5.4-1, Table 5.4-2, and Table 5.4-3; sources: nuscale_ch05_0020, nuscale_ch05_0019).

no_browse_first.answer: The total net electrical output of a full 12-module NuScale plant is approximately 570 MWe (megawatts electric) (nuscale_ch01_0006; nuscale_ch01_0004; nuscale_ch01_0008; nuscale_ch01_0005).

The NuScale Reactor Coolant System (RCS) has two reactor safety valves (RSVs).  
Source: Figure 5.1-2 (Reactor Coolant System Simplified Diagram) and Table 5.2-2 (Reactor Safety Valves - Design Parameters).

The NuScale RCS uses natural circulation, where heated coolant rises through the central riser above the core due to reduced density, flows through the steam generators where it cools and descends in the downcomer, with the riser providing the necessary elevation and separation to establish and sustain this flow loop by maximizing the driving force from density differences (see Figures 5.1-1, 5.1-3, and 1.2-7; nuscale_ch05_0003; nuscale_ch05_0004; nuscale_ch01_0018).

The NuScale seismic design in Ch.01 requires safety-related RCS components to meet ASME BPVC Class 1 standards and seismic qualification per RG 1.100, ensuring that RCS components (e.g., pressurizer, steam generators) in Ch.05 are designed, fabricated, and inspected to withstand seismic events as part of Seismic Category I structures.  
Sources: nuscale_ch01_0150; nuscale_ch05_0008; nuscale_ch05_0011; nuscale_ch05_0019; Table 1.9-3; Table 5.2-6; Table 5.2-7; Figure 5.1-2; Figure 5.4-17.

The NuScale natural circulation driving force is much lower than the forced circulation in a conventional PWR, as NuScale relies solely on passive density differences for coolant flow (with no pumps), resulting in lower flow rates and temperature differentials (e.g., NuScale core ΔT at 100% power is 99.8°F with 587 kg/s flow, compared to much higher forced flows in conventional PWRs); thus, regulatory criteria for forced flow and pump failures are not applicable to NuScale’s design.  
Sources: nuscale_ch01_0730, nuscale_ch05_0003, Table 5.1-2, Table 1.3-1.

The SG (Steam Generator) design parameters (Table 5.4-2) are: design pressure 2100 psia, design temperature 650°F, tube wall outer diameter 0.625 in, and total heat transfer area 17,928 ft²; the RPV (Reactor Pressure Vessel) design parameters (Table 5.3-1) are: design pressure 2100 psia, design temperature 650°F, overall height 778 in, and inside diameter (lower section) 96.5 in—both components share the same design pressure and temperature, but differ in geometry and function.  
Source: Table 5.4-2, Table 5.3-1.

The NuScale RPV has an inside diameter of 107.5 inches and an overall height of 778 inches, compared to a typical PWR RPV with an inside diameter of 173 inches; NuScale’s RPV dimensions from Table 5.3-1 and Figure 5.3-1 confirm its much smaller, more compact vessel relative to conventional PWRs, as summarized in Table 1.3-1.  
(Source: Table 1.3-1, Table 5.3-1, Figure 5.3-1)

Yes, the minimum design flow rate of 538.5 kg/s at 100% power is adequate for core cooling, as confirmed by the text discussion and Table 5.1-2, which show that this flow rate maintains core temperature and thermal margins within safety limits under natural circulation conditions (see Table 5.1-2 and nuscale_ch05_0006).

Yes, the integral steam generator (SG) design described in both Ch.01 and Ch.05 is adequate for addressing steam generator tube rupture (SGTR) concerns, as it features robust helical tube construction, validated support structures, low corrosion risk, and is supported by passive safety systems (DHRS) that meet regulatory radiological and safety criteria for SGTR events (nuscale_ch05_0021; nuscale_ch05_0022; nuscale_ch01_0662; nuscale_ch01_0663).
